data.title: AMD: An AI Giant In The Making
data.description:
I have been bullish on Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) for a good part of this year and it has worked out very well even after the recent pullback. In early September, I reiterated my buy rating and since then the stock has rallied another ~35% and so I believe it would be an understatement to say that the bulls are pleased. The company reported earnings back in November and today, we'll see if it's time to double down or take some profits.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Q3 was generally a good quarter for AMD with Data Center revenues accelerating. While there was some profitability weakness and Q4 guidance is a bit mixed, the long term outlook offered at their Financial Analyst Day shows that the company is an AI giant in the making. Therefore, I'm fazed by the elevated valuation and in fact view the recent pullback as a prime buying opportunity.

Revenues Show Robust Demand

For 2025 Q3, AMD reported record revenues of $9.2 billion, representing a growth rate of 35.59%. They stated that this strength was driven by strong demand in their Data Center AI, Server, and PC businesses and so key parts of the company are performing well. As you can see in the chart above, growth accelerated from the previous quarter and is basically at the highest level in the past few years. Therefore, I would say that this indicates worries about a slowdown are misplaced at this point. In addition, AMD was able to breeze past expectations by a margin of nearly $500 million and so overall it was a comfortable quarter for the company. Let's now turn to a closer look at their all-important Data Center segment.

Data Center Growth Accelerates

AMD's Data Center business is its largest by revenue and also the most important for the company's strategic positioning. However, it actually isn't the company's largest growth driver currently. For Q3, they reported $4.3 billion in revenues representing growth of 22% as shown above. Note that this is well below the company-wide growth rate discussed above. On the other hand, their Client and Gaming segment saw 73% growth to $4 billion in revenue. Ryzen processors saw record sales and Gaming revenue was up 181% YoY due to robust demand for Radeon GPUs. This is really quite impressive given the consumer sentiment right now but in the long run, there is no question that their Data Center business is the core focus for AMD.

While the 22% growth rate may not seem overly impressive, it actually represents a significant acceleration from Q2's 14% increase and so momentum is strong for this key business. It is already stated in the infographic that they saw strong demand for their Instinct MI350 Series GPUs but AMD was able to secure a strategic partnership with OpenAI that will initially see them rollout 1GW of compute in the form of Instinct MI450 GPUs in the second half of next year. The partnership is for 6GW in total and so for investors that are concerned about Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO) eating their lunch, the market seems large enough for multiple companies to be successful. The demand for compute really does seem to be insatiable right now. Lastly, you can see that operating margin dipped significantly from the prior year period with operating expenses weighing. We'll discus their company-wide efficiency and profitability below but this could be a potential concern if R&D isn't the driver of this increase in expenses.

Profitability Is Decent But Not Great

In terms of profitability, AMD's results were respectable but not too impressive. As shown above, gross margin didn't change YoY and remains at 54% and so cost control and pricing power have been largely stable. This definitely isn't a bad thing as a gross margin in the mid-50s percentage range is quite strong.

However, as you can see, operating expenses as a percentage of revenue increased by 2 percentage points YoY. That led to operating margin contraction of 1 percentage point to 24%. While this operating margin is also still quite strong, contraction is never desirable. As stated above, if R&D spending led to this lower profitability, it wouldn't be an issue for me. However, looking at their income statement, R&D only increased by 31% to $2.139 billion, less than the percentage increase in revenue. Therefore, corporate efficiency could have been even better in my view. That may be forgivable since the company is trying to rapidly scale their AI solutions.

At the end of the day, AMD reported an adjusted EPS of $1.20, up 30% YoY. This growth rate lags the top line growth rate by a notable margin to demonstrate some shakiness in profitability but still they managed to beat EPS expectations by $0.03. Therefore, it isn't a major worry at this point.

Near Term Outlook

AMD provided guidance for Q4 in their earnings release. With revenues being expected to be $9.6 billion at the midpoint, they are implying growth of 25% YoY. That is a notable slowdown from Q3's growth rate but it is unsure where this deceleration will originate from. If its from their Client & Gaming segment, it would be much less of a worry than if it was from their Data Center segment. In their earnings call, the company did state that they expect gaming revenues to be down strong double digits QoQ and so it seems that the weakness will not be from their key Data Center business.

For gross margin, they are expecting 54.5% and that would be a minimal change from the year ago quarter's 54%. Therefore, again pricing power and cost control will remain rather stable.

Lastly, they project operating expenses of $2.8 billion, representing a 32% rise. With that outpacing the projected top line growth rate, profitability is expected to remain under some pressure in the near term. Overall, while their revenue guidance was above consensus expectations, I would say that the outlook for Q4 is a little mixed.

Long Term Outlook

On their Financial Analyst Day, AMD laid out some very ambitious targets and if they are able to deliver, the company is set to become a computing giant. We'll focus on their Data Center business once again for this discussion. Firstly, they reported that Instinct MI350 Series GPUs are already the "fastest ramping product in company history." Moving forward their Instinct MI450 Series GPUs "are expected to deliver rack-scale performance leadership with industry leading memory capacity and scale-out bandwidth beginning in the third quarter of 2026." Furthermore, the release of MI500 is set for 2027 and so the company clearly has a strong pipeline. In addition to just GPUs, the company's next-gen "Venice" CPUs and networking solutions are high growth opportunities in the data center space. Overall, I believe AMD is positioning itself to be a major player in cutting edge AI compute as they seem to be investing heavily in innovation.

On the financial side of things, for the long term AMD is targeting a 35%+ revenue CAGR with operating margin of over 35%. For their Data Center business in particular, they are aiming for a 60% revenue CAGR. Firstly, both the company-wide and segment specific targets are clearly ambitious and secondly, you can see it is true that in the long run, their Data Center segment is expected to lead the way even though growth lagged their Client & Gaming segment in Q3. For more details on their long term targets, I encourage readers to take a look at their Financial Analyst Day press release but I believe this discussion has been enough to show that the long term growth story of the company remains highly intact at this point.

Valuation Is High But Justified

In my previous article, the forward P/E was reported to be 41.35 and so there has been significant expansion since then to the current reading of 54.24. While the valuation is now a bit elevated, I believe there are strong justifications for this.

In today's analysis, the company has been shown to be overall robust. Sure, there are some profitability pressures but Data Center acceleration is the key takeaway. Furthermore, while I found their Q4 guidance to be a bit mixed, the long term outlook they showed at their Financial Analyst Day proves that short-termism is unwise. The long term growth story is highly intact and if things go anywhere near as good as planned, AMD is set to become a major player in the AI compute space.

Therefore, I believe the valuation deserves to be where it is. In fact, I view the recent pullback as an attractive entry point as fears of a slowdown seem to be overblown.

Conclusion

Let's cut to the chase. AMD is currently seen mainly as an alternative to Nvidia or Broadcom in the AI chip space but there are concrete signs that it will join them in becoming a giant down the road. As stated above, Data Center revenues are accelerating and more importantly, management is clearly confident about their long term ambitions. The MI450 and MI500 are set to push the boundaries of GPU performance and their direction for CPUs and networking is also ambitious. With the outlook as bright as ever for the company, I see the valuation as being justified and the recent pullback due to the November tech scare may in fact be a great buying opportunity. Therefore, I'm reiterating my bullish rating on AMD stock.

data.title: SOXQ: The Semiconductor ETF Riding The AI Boom
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Invesco PHLX Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SOXQ) warrants a buy rating due to its mix of top holdings that are postured to capture significant AI investment and growth. As a result, the fund will continue seeing market-beating returns despite a relatively high valuation. Finally, SOXQ offers investors with the lowest fees of many popular semiconductor-focused peer ETFs.

Invesco PHLX Semiconductor ETF – Overview and Compared ETFs

SOXQ is an ETF that seeks to capture the 30 largest U.S. semiconductor companies. The fund has 31 holdings and $765M in AUM. Despite its stated objective and 88% weight in the United States, SOXQ does include holdings in Taiwan, the Netherlands, and Israel. Despite being a relatively new ETF, initiated in 2021, SOXQ has been delivering strong returns which will likely continue for reasons I will discuss in this article.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are Invesco Semiconductors ETF (PSI), VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH), and State Street SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD). PSI is another Invesco fund that offers a more evenly distributed weight on holdings with a selection method including price momentum, quality, and value. SMH has the fewest holdings and is the most heavily concentrated semiconductor ETF compared. Finally, XSD uses a modified equal-weight index and therefore has the most even blend across large, mid, and small cap semiconductor stocks.

SOXQ: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

SOXQ has seen an impressive 3-year average annual return of 45.94%. By comparison, PSI has a 3-year average return of 36.07%. SMH has seen the best performance with a 3-year average return of 57.68%. XSD, with a modified equal-weight index, has seen the lowest 3-year average return with an average 31.36% return. All funds have outperformed the S&P 500 Index in recent years.

Many industry-specific funds have higher fees than broad-based ETFs. SOXQ is no exception but still has a very affordable 0.19% expense ratio, the lowest of the semiconductor ETFs compared. While SOXQ captures growth-focused holdings that are not significant dividend providers, the fund does include a modest dividend yield of 0.52%. While minimal, this yield is higher than other compared semiconductor funds.

SOXQ Holdings and Its Competitive Advantage

Due to its industry-specific focus, SOXQ is concentrated with over 64% weight in its top 10 holdings. Additionally, almost 30% of its weight is on just top three holdings of Broadcom (AVGO), Nvidia (NVDA), and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The downside of this concentration is a 5-year standard deviation of 24.4, significantly higher than the overall market as measured by the S&P 500 Index.

While pure concentration on semiconductor companies introduces risk to SOXQ as I will discuss later, the strengths of each holding will continue to drive superior returns. This is particularly true for SOXQ’s top three holdings, due to their unique growth characteristics. Each of these factors are covered in further detail below.

Broadcom: The Momentum Shows No Signs of Stopping

The first strong driver of capital appreciation for SOXQ is its top holding, Broadcom, at over 10% weight. The global tech company has a track record of tremendous growth through a string of successful acquisitions. With strategic acquisitions of companies like VMware, Broadcom has achieved a 28% YoY revenue growth and high profitability with a 31% net income margin.

Looking forward, Broadcom’s key strategic advance is its linkages to Google and OpenAI by supplying application-specific chips or ASICs. The development of these AI accelerators, or XPUs, makes up 65% of Broadcom’s semiconductor revenue. A downside for AVGO is its forward P/E of about 60, significantly higher than Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices. However, with the XPU industry expecting 23% additional growth in 2026 along with AVGO’s key partnerships with Google and OpenAI, this valuation is not unjustified.

Nvidia: Massive AI Expenditure by Tech Mega-Caps

The second key factor for SOXQ is its #2 holding, Nvidia, at almost 10% weight. Nvidia’s data center graphic processing units, or GPUs, supply other mega-cap tech companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), and Alphabet (GOOG), (GOOGL). This production, in turn, is a primary driver for the company’s revenue. As a result, Nvidia’s data centers for computing saw 55% YoY growth in revenue while its networking data centers saw a 164% increase in YoY revenue growth.

Nvidia’s future growth can be traced to the anticipated AI investment in massive tech companies. Investment in Nvidia’s chips comprised nearly 47% of Microsoft’s capital expenditures and about 25% of Meta’s capital expenditures. This staggering spending will continue to fuel Nvidia looking forward due to the company’s chip production capacity. Therefore, NVDA’s forward P/E of 38 is also reasonable.

AMD: Overlooked Gaming Segment Seeing Strong Growth

The third strength for SOXQ is its #3 holding, AMD, at over 8% weight. AMD’s data center segment is up 22% YoY due to demand for its processors and GPUs. However, data centers are not the most exciting segment for the SOXQ holding. While data centers accounted for about 80% of AMD’s revenue in 2024, the gaming and client segment has since taken up a significant portion of revenue. Due to AMD’s successful processors for gaming, this segment saw a 73% YoY increase in revenue.

AMD is strongly positioned to continue to benefit from the growing global gaming industry. With an expected 13% CAGR through the end of the decade, the global gaming industry will continue to need high-quality processors supporting 3D graphics and multiplayer functionality. A downside of AMD is its current valuation with a forward P/E of 55. Looking forward, AMD is also boosted by its recent partnership with OpenAI with multi-gigawatt support to Open AI’s next generation AI infrastructure. AMD will install their first GPU to support this agreement in late 2026.

Current Valuation

Despite a 13% return since late last month, SOXQ is still 1.7% from its all-time high seen in October. All funds have significantly outperformed the S&P 500 Index this year, consistent with the long-term trend. However, the forward P/E for Broadcom and AMD, in particular, has increased the valuation for SOXQ overall.

As a result of recent performance, SOXQ has the highest valuation of the compared semiconductor funds as measured by its P/E ratio. However, given the significant AI investment and growth characteristics for SOXQ’s top holdings, the fund’s current valuation does not negate a buy rating overall for the fund.

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 2 Dec 25

Risks to Investors

The foremost risk to investors in SOXQ is its heavy concentration solely the semiconductor industry. While SOXQ and peer semiconductor funds have seen market-beating returns for the past several years, critics point towards the “circular investment” between mega-cap tech companies, fomenting an “AI bubble” that could burst.

This circular investment includes OpenAI’s stake in AMD, for example, totaling 10% for the partnership previously discussed. However, fears are overblown as only about 41% of Nvidia’s revenue comes from mega-cap tech companies like Amazon, Meta, and Alphabet. Additionally, the forward PE ratio of the IT sector of the S&P 500 Index during the dot-com bubble reached over 50. Today, the IT sector’s P/E is just over 30. However, other indicators such as the concentration of the largest meg-caps in the S&P 500 and the disparity between U.S. small caps versus large caps is also reason for concern looking forward.

Concluding Summary

SOXQ and compared funds have all seen incredible returns over the past few years. This performance has resulted in a relatively high valuation. However, SOXQ still warrants a buy rating, despite its current P/E, due to the strong growth characteristics of its top holdings. While SOXQ’s concentration on relatively few holdings presents risks, the development of AI will rely on the products created by SOXQ’s top holdings.

data.title: Mastercard: Time To Buy This Dividend Growth Titan
data.description:
As an investor, I like to own companies that are embedded in everyday life. This is because consistent use of a company's products and/or services often translates into reliable revenue and earnings growth.

In turn, this can also support strong payout growth. Since I would eventually like the option to live off my passive income, this is critical for me.

That brings me to my topic of today, which is Mastercard (MA). Whether it's essentials like health insurance/health care services or discretionary items like occasionally eating out, my favorite way to pay is with credit cards (and to pay them off in full each month, of course). This is a way to earn 2% to 3% back on purchases I was going to make anyway, so that makes it an easy decision for me.

When I last covered Mastercard with a buy rating in September, I liked its reputation for steady double earnings beats. The company winning deals and building acceptance of its products in key markets was another plus. Mastercard's A+ S&P credit rating was a positive, too. Shares seemed to be slightly discounted as well.

Two months later, I'm reiterating my buy rating. Mastercard is still cutting deals to position its business for further growth. The company's net leverage ratio is very low. Finally, the stock is priced at a moderate discount to my fair value estimate.

Mastercard Had A Great Q3

On October 30th, Mastercard released its third-quarter earnings report for the period ended September 30th, 2025. The Purchase, New York-based company's net revenue vaulted 16.7% higher over the year-ago period to $8.60 billion in the quarter (including a 2% tailwind from favorable foreign currency translation). That topped the analyst consensus by $70 million during the quarter. This marked the 20th quarter in as many quarters that Mastercard exceeded the analyst consensus for net revenue.

So, how did Mastercard log healthy top-line growth for the third quarter? Well, the company's total cardholder base expanded by 6.3% year-over-year to over 3.6 billion cards. As has been the case in the past, a combination of execution, international adoption, and the network effect powered this cardholder growth in the quarter.

Additionally, Mastercard's cardholder base continued to spend. This fueled a 9% growth rate in the company's gross dollar volume during the quarter. A growing cardholder base and steady consumer spending also led switched transactions to rise by 10% for the quarter. Lastly, continued growth in travel and nontravel-related cross-border spending led the metric to jump 15% higher over the year-ago period in the quarter.

Mastercard's adjusted diluted EPS surged 12.6% year-over-year to $4.38 during the third quarter. That was $0.07 greater than the analyst consensus for the quarter. As was the case for net revenue, this was also the 20th quarter out of the past 20 that Mastercard beat the analyst consensus. A slight contraction in the non-GAAP net profit margin to 46% in the quarter more than offset a 2.2% decrease in the outstanding share count. That's why adjusted diluted EPS growth lagged net revenue growth during the quarter.

Mastercard's business update through October 28th points to a stable growth outlook. Switched volume for the first four weeks of Q4 was up 9%, which was similar to the switched volume growth posted in Q3 2025. The same can be said for switched transactions and cross-border volume, which were also about in line sequentially with Q3.

From an executional standpoint, Mastercard is also doing well. In his remarks during the Q3 2025 Earnings Call, CEO Michael Miebach pointed to a drumbeat of wins to support its operational strength. These included multiple co-brand wins with major airlines and retailers, such as Japan Airlines and Uni-President Group in Taiwan.

In the Middle East, Mastercard's value proposition has helped it to win affluent portfolios around the globe. These include First Abu Dhabi Bank in the UAE, Saudi Awwal Bank/Saudi National Bank, and Doha Bank in Qatar.

Longer term, Mastercard has tangible growth tailwinds, too. For one, there's the fact that inflation helps its payment volumes to organically grow over time. Then, there's the network effect combined with operational execution that's assisting Mastercard with cardholder base growth.

Finally, there's the fact that the global consumer secular opportunity remains around $11 trillion in gross dollar volume. For an idea of just how large this opportunity is, it's even bigger than the $10.4 trillion in TTM GDV processed by the company.

Mastercard is working to move into high-value, recurring verticals like rent. Rental payments globally are mostly paid via check or ACH. Recently, Mastercard partnered with Renti, a rental management platform in New Zealand. This is a more recent example of how Mastercard is penetrating such markets, unlocking even more value for shareholders.

That's why I believe the FAST Graphs analyst consensus estimates for adjusted diluted EPS are realistic. For 2025, the prediction is for adjusted diluted EPS to grow by 13% to $16.50. In 2026, another 15.8% increase in adjusted diluted EPS to $19.11 is the current analyst consensus. For 2027, an additional 16.2% rise in adjusted diluted EPS to $22.20 is the current forecast.

Mastercard's financial condition is also enviable. As of September 30th, 2025, the company held $8.3 billion in net debt. Against approximately $19.8 billion in annualized EBITDA through the first nine months of 2025, this is a net debt-to-leverage ratio of just 0.4x. That backs up the A+ S&P credit rating on a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were according to Mastercard's Q3 2025 Earnings Press Release, Mastercard's Q3 2025 Earnings Presentation, and Mastercard's Q3 2025 Supplemental Operational Performance Data).

Fair Value Has Breached $600 A Share

In the last couple of months, Mastercard's shares have declined by 2%. All the while, the S&P 500 index (SP500) has edged 2% higher over that time.

This has arguably made Mastercard an even more appealing investment. That dip and the passage of time (e.g., higher adjusted diluted EPS estimates and weighting shifting more into 2026) have pushed the forward 12-month P/E ratio down to 28.8. This is a notable improvement versus the already reasonable forward 12-month P/E ratio of 30.9 in late September.

Looking ahead, I'm maintaining my fair value P/E ratio of 32. That would be just below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 33.7, which arguably incorporates some conservatism into my fair value estimate.

This is especially the case when considering that Mastercard's growth profile is arguably intact. The company's forward annual adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus of 15.9% is about in line with the 10-year average CAGR of 16.8%.

As I alluded to earlier, Mastercard's profitability is also holding steady in the mid- to high-40% range. That demonstrates its fundamentals are about as strong now as they have been in recent years.

Currently, the calendar year 2025 is 92% complete. This means there's another 8% of 2025 and 92% of 2026 yet to come in the next 12 months. That yields a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $18.90.

Applying my fair value multiple of 32, I compute a fair value of $605 a share. Relative to the current $545 share price (as of December 2nd, 2025), this equates to a 10% discount to fair value.

If Mastercard returns to fair value and matches the growth consensus, it could deliver a 14% total return by the end of 2026. Through 2030, the stock could deliver 15% annual total returns.

Mastercard Has The Makings Of A Future Dividend Aristocrat

Mastercard's 0.6% dividend yield remains well below the financial sector's median forward yield of 3%. This is a modest 10 basis point narrowing of the spread since September. Still, Mastercard's grade for overall dividend yield remains an F from Seeking Alpha's Quant System.

The payment processing giant is still one of my favorite dividend growers in my portfolio, however. Mastercard has upped its dividend for 14 consecutive years, which is head and shoulders above the sector median of 3 years. That's why the Quant System issues a B+ grade for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from September).

Mastercard's dividend is also perfectly positioned to keep growing. The company's adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio is poised to be in the high-teens percentage range in 2026. This would be substantially below the 60% payout ratio that rating agencies prefer for the industry, per the Zen Research Terminal.

That explains the very low dividend cut risks for Mastercard. The Zen Research Terminal anticipates a 0.3% risk of a dividend reduction over the next 12 months and a 1.3% probability in the next average recession. These data points support the A+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety (the same as it was in September).

This is why I believe that any day now, Mastercard will lift its quarterly dividend per share by 14.5% to $0.87. That would be consistent with the five-year annual dividend growth rate of 13.7% (which itself was more than double the sector median of 6.3% over that time). This is sufficient for the Quant System to issue an A+ grade for overall dividend growth (unchanged from September as well).

Risks To Consider

Mastercard is a fundamentally thriving business. Yet, some risks could materially impact the investment thesis.

The regulatory front comes with a set of risks to Mastercard.

The European Commission's probe into Visa (V) and Mastercard, which I have cited in recent articles, is still ongoing as of now. Inquiries in questionnaires sent to retailers and merchants back in May included the number of fees or services charged by the duo in recent years, the number of changes, and the number of new fees or services introduced.

The risk surrounding this remains the potential for fines of up to 10% of global revenue. If that happens, it could meaningfully weigh on Mastercard's earnings results.

Domestically, Visa and Mastercard frequently face scrutiny from politicians over their success in the marketplace. The latest example came when Illinois Senator Dick Durbin opposed the proposed settlement of Visa and Mastercard with merchants. This would reduce the U.S. combined average effective credit interchange by 0.1% for five years, as well as give merchants more control over the kinds of credit cards to accept.

Another risk to Mastercard is that the sheer scale of its payment network attracts a never-ending barrage of attempted cyber breaches. If any attempted hacks find major success, the sensitive stakeholder information could be jeopardized. This could lead to regulatory penalties and lawsuits, resulting in a loss of trust among stakeholders. That could make it more difficult for the company to attract card issuers, merchants, and cardholders in the future, which could limit its growth prospects.

Finally, Mastercard's growth somewhat depends on a healthy economy. This is why it makes sense to watch U.S. economic data in the weeks and months ahead. The most recent betting odds from Kalshi peg a U.S. recession at just 4% for 2025. So, while the near-term risk is limited, the next economic slowdown could also cause a brief and temporary deceleration in Mastercard's net revenue and adjusted diluted EPS growth rates.

Summary: A World-Class Dividend Grower On Sale Now

Mastercard is a phenomenal company. New client wins and a business model that benefits from inflation are key growth drivers. Mastercard is financially stable as well. There looks to be plenty of room for further dividend growth as well.

Sealing the buy case is the fact that the stock is trading at an appealing valuation. Shares are priced at a 10% discount to fair value, which is why I'm sticking with my buy rating. In closing, that is why I'm comfortable with Mastercard comprising 1.3% of my portfolio, making it my 23rd-largest holding.

data.title: Protara Therapeutics: End Of 2025 Proving Eventful Indeed
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

Earlier this year, I prepared an update to the Protara Therapeutics (TARA) thesis, maintaining optimism for their bladder cancer pipeline hoping to address specific drug shortages in the non-metastatic setting, as well as a novel indication. Now, we have a key readout in bladder cancer to digest, with management commentary providing more color for a path forward for their main agent in development. Today, I want to showcase why there may be reason for ongoing optimism, even as the stock has doubled since my last "Buy" recommendation.

Pipeline Updates

TARA-002

In Q4 so far, we have seen two important trial readouts related to TARA-002, a powdered streptococcus bacterium being explored in the settings of non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) and lymphatic malformations. We've previously seen 6-month complete response rates of 72% in their phase 2 ADVANCED-2 trial, with an update at AUA 2025 pushing that CR rate to 76% at any time during treatment in the first 21 evaluable patients. This included several patients who were unresponsive to prior BCG therapy.

On December 3, we got another interim update of ADVANCED-2. Now we have data from 31 patients who were BCG-naive receiving TARA-002. Of the 26 evaluable patients, 69% achieved a complete response at 6 months, suggesting that the response rate is holding up over time. Of the 14 patients with sufficient follow-up, 7 had a CR lasting at least 12 months. The company is also continuing to enroll BCG-unresponsive patients, with an update in a registrational cohort of 25 patients anticipated in Q1 2026.

TARA communicated in the update call that they believe themselves to be in alignment with the FDA on having this BCG-unresponsive group of patients be a registrational cohort supporting approval if the trial shows compelling data. They've also gotten written feedback from the FDA on the design of a trial that would be suitable to pursue an approval in BCG-naive patients.

We've also seen a recent update for the phase 2 STARBORN-1 trial exploring TARA-002 in lymphatic malformations. Of 12 patients who had received at least one dose by the data cutoff, 8 were evaluable for efficacy, all of whom achieved "clinical success" at the eight-week response assessment, defined as a 90% or greater reduction of their malformation volume from baseline.

TARA intends to use these data to begin conversations with the FDA to figure out a path to approval. TARA management estimates that there are around 20,000 patients actively seeking treatment for lymphatic malformations, and there are no FDA-approved systemic therapies available to treat these.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, TARA held $12.6 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $120.1 million in marketable securities. Operating expenses reached $14.8 million, and after interest, the net loss for the quarter was $13.3 million.

The net cash used for operations in the first 9 months of 2025 was $39.4 million, implying an annualized cash burn rate of $52.5 million. At this rate, TARA's operational runway is approximately 2 to 2.5 years. This is consistent with management guidance that they support planned operations "into mid-2027."

Strengths and Risks

TARA is continuing to build out a promising case for TARA-002 in two different important indications. Of course, it's not a done deal, and there are risks to consider alongside the boons from these data.

Strength - The seeds of success are sprouting quickly

In my last article, I highlighted that there have been some very promising early signals of efficacy, but that we need to avoid getting ahead of ourselves. I indicated that we were still in "early days" for TARA-002. With this more robust readout and longer follow-up, we're getting an indication that the promising high complete response rates are holding up, and that they are quite durable, as well.

By the end of Q1 2026, we may very well have a clear indication as to whether they're going to be able to apply for approval successfully, although we do not have a clear timeline for when that would be coming.

Strength - Well capitalized to reach key milestones

With over 2 years of cash on hand, however, we can afford to ride out data updates and regulatory conversations without needing to raise significant funding. This raises my hopes for TARA to be able to get to a catalyst that serves as a position of strength for a definitive cash raise or serious partnership.

Risk - Brewing peers may impact the potential market

Although TARA-002 is being positioned as a potential replacement for BCG in the management of NMIBC, it is worth pointing out that we've seen significant evolution in the standard of care for this disease state. We've seen the approval of Anktiva in BCG-unresponsive disease, for example. EnGene Therapeutics is also positioning itself well with a gene therapy in BCG-unresponsive disease. UroGen's mitomycin formulation has also recently entered the fray for NMIBC.

None of these are necessarily direct competitors at this time, but it does signal a widening market that may be harder to break into. When you consider that many of these indications for the novel therapies are in BCG-unresponsive disease, that could contribute a lot toward continuing to make BCG the standard, since it fits neatly within the treatment sequence.

Bottom-Line Summary

The TARA investment thesis relies on one's conviction that a market cap (at the time of writing) of around $240 million is unjustified with the potential indications for TARA-002. This is a little less than double their liquidity, so the market has come to see some promise in the pipeline.

Considering the data are continuing to evolve positively in NMIBC, and that we're seeing some encouraging signals for lymphatic malformations, it is looking more likely that TARA can tap into some large markets (NMIBC itself is estimated at over $2 billion per year), and their potential to be a replacement for BCG, which remains in seriously short supply, may give them important inroads into carving out a niche in that market.

Given this recent update, I continue to hold a "Buy" sentiment on TARA, although with less conviction than my last article. As with any of these fast upward movements, there is plenty of room for downside moves, as well. Last December, we saw the stock price more than double on an earlier readout in NMIBC. And then the market began a months-long decline in sentiment.

But the more we learn, the more that TARA-002 is looking like the real deal. Of course, they're not there yet, but TARA is continuing to justify a bullish thesis, in my opinion.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: The MLP To Buy Before 2026
data.description:
Introduction

A lot has happened for Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE: EPD) since my last analysis on the company and I was actually surprised that I did not review my thesis earlier. Since my last analysis, the company has appreciated by a total return of around 7%, actually making it an outperformer in the midstream industry.

With the slew of earnings and strategic deals that the company has done, I want to assess if my Strong Buy case is still relevant with the current O&G dynamics and if it is still the safest bet in the industry.

Current Dynamics

I'll first dive into the most recent earnings with EPD actually reporting a mixed set, with a beat on revenue at $12.02b while EPS missed by nearly 9% at 61 cents. And though the company missed on earnings, I believe that the underlying operational volumes were relatively strong in a similar fashion to other midstream companies such as Energy Transfer. EPD set nine new operational records thanks to strong demand across its vast integrated system primarily in natural gas processing and NGL pipeline volumes, which increased by around 9% Y/Y. There were some slight headwinds, mainly lower sales margins in the NGL market as well as some downtime, but overall, the quarter was sound and was overly positive on the company's outlook.

This has pushed EPD to boost its quarterly distribution to around $0.545 per common unit, which is an increase of 3.8% Y/Y. Furthermore, the DCF coverage ratio is relatively healthy at around 1.5x thanks to a total DCF of $1.8B. The company also increased its buyback authorization to $5B, up from an initial $2B. And on a reverse note, EPD reduced its CapEx guidance for 2026 to between $2.2B and $2.5B, which is a 50% reduction compared to 2025. This is mainly due to the company actually signalling the end of its multi-year construction cycle. The direct consequence should be a drastic increase in the partnership's FCF, as stated by A.J. Teague, the company's Co-CEO. Thus, coupled with the record volumes, we should see a great 2026 for the company's profitability.

On another note, I feel like EPD has been highly focused on asserting its dominance over the Permian's NGL and Natural gas value chain as it made some strategic acquisitions and major JV expansions. First, the Oxy acquisition and processing deal directly improved EPD's position in the Midland Basin. The deal came at a value of around $580MM in cash to acquire some natural gas gathering systems and around 200 miles of Nat gas gathering pipelines. The rationale behind the deal would be that it supports Oxy's production and allows EPD to have access to around 1,000 drillable locations, as well as give long-term contract agreements between the two major energy players. Following this, EPD has announced that it will build a new Athena natural gas processing plant to handle the increased volume from the deal.

Furthermore, EPD and ExxonMobil just recently announced that they will sign a partnership agreement for EPD's Bahia Natural Gas Liquids NGL Pipeline, in which Exxon will acquire around 40% of the project. This will translate into a contribution of around $650MM to cover its proportional share of the costs incurred to date on the 550-mile pipeline that is currently being commissioned to transport 600,000 barrels per day of mixed NGLs from the Permian to EPD's fractionation hub. In all, I believe that this was a good deal as it derisks the project for EPD and secures a massive anchor shipper in Exxon Mobil, thus immediately greenlighting an expansion that will capture a projected 30%+ growth in the Permian NGL production by 2030.

Valuation

Currently, EPD has an EV/EBITDA of 11.14, one of the lowest among its peers, beaten only by one of my holdings, Energy Transfer (ET). With the investment cycle being finished, I expect EBIDTA to pick up in 2026 and probably reach over $11B based on the current volume throughput. I also expect the company's FCF yield to pick up as the company tones down CapEx, raising the current yield of 4.4%, making the company even more interesting on a valuation standpoint. And though generally EPD has been pricier thanks to its secure operation in general, mainly compared to peers, we can see that there is a sort of discount currently for EPD, offering a buying opportunity for income investors. Mainly, the current distribution yield stands at 6.64% and should drastically rise in the coming years as EPD reduces its CapEx, thus increasing Distributable Cash Flow.

On the growth front, with the company's capital being heavily employed in the Permian and the Gulf Coast export capacity, I believe that the company's future is heavily tied to the highest-growth regions in North America. EPD has become a dominant player in the Permian, mainly in NGL and gas processing, which should continue to have a significant importance as transition fuel. EPD is clearly cementing its NGL domination with ongoing strong demand going through its Mont Belvieu facility, which is undergoing further expansion that should well tie into the durable EBITDA growth as it comes online. I'll say that despite EPD being known for its stability, right now could be a great moment for its growth prospects as well.

Though margins have been under relative pressure over the past couple of quarters, they remain highly stable thanks to the business model and EBITDA margin is picking back up towards 20% as fee-based contracts are currently accounting for around 82% of sales for the past 9 months. As I mentioned earlier, I expect margins and overall profitability to pick back up for Enterprise, with, for example, the Fractionator 14 ramping up, boosting processing margins for the remainder of the year and into 2026. Though the core margin growth will come from the $5.1B realized in growth projects coming online, that should push EPD's profitability back in line with peers. Though right now, EPD has an excellent ROE, higher than Kinder Morgan or even Enbridge at 19.7% thanks to the company's highly efficient operation.

Risks

There is always the risk tied to producers that even with EPD's operation being insulated from commodity price volatility, customers are not. If prices fall too drastically, producers could simply cut their production, reducing throughput through EPD's system. Though NGL marketing, processing margin, and the overall petrochemical segment are exposed to price volatility.

And though the arms race has toned down for midstream companies, there is still aggressive competition mainly to get a piece of the Permian pie and with all of that CapEx spent on the area, it could lead to excess pipeline and processing capacity across the industry.

Conclusion

At $32 a unit, I still believe that Enterprise is well worth owning in an income-focused portfolio. It is probably the most stable operation in the midstream, and the premium that you used to pay for that security blanket is currently eroded, leaving a great opportunity for investors. We should see a high uptick in profitability in 2026, pushing unit prices to new heights, in my opinion. I keep my Strong Buy rating on Enterprise Products Partners.

data.title: USA: Hold While AI Bubble Fears Loom
data.description:
Liberty All-Star Equity (USA) stood out to me because of its double-digit yield from an equity fund. With a long-term performance comparable to the S&P 500, it's not a bad investment, it's been suitable income for the past decade. Nevertheless, I think risks related to an AI bubble in its tech positions are too great without a stronger NAV discount first.

Concept of the Fund

USA is intended to be an income fund, regularly distributing dividends to shareholders. As its most recent annual report explains:

The current policy is to pay distributions on its shares totaling approximately 10 percent of its net asset value per year, payable in four quarterly installments of 2.5 percent of the Fund’s net asset value at the close of the New York Stock Exchange on the Friday prior to each quarterly declaration date.

Put in plainer terms, USA does not primarily support the distributions with underlying cash flow, such as interest or dividends. Rather, it will simply distribute a portion of its NAV, and this is only supported as long as the underlying investments perform well.

The tables above show how the portfolio is invested to achieve this. The top ten holdings reveal many large cap stocks, including some Mag 7 names.

This investment selection is a product of a five-way split in the portfolio management, which it considers three parts "value" and two parts "growth." Each manager allocates roughly an equal portion of the assets based on those value and growth preferences.

The result is a portfolio that is similar to the S&P 500 in many respects, but active selection limits the portfolio to 139 holdings, with less concentration in its top ten picks, and lower multiples to earnings and book. The portfolio dividend yield, at 1.4%, only accounts for a small portion of the distributions, meaning that assets must be trimmed each quarter to maintain the distribution.

While this is an atypical income fund, this doesn't mean it's incapable of distributing, and there are several years of results to give insights into this strategy.

Historical Results

The first thing to consider is its distribution history. Over the past decade, USA has paid dividends every year, even in the midst of COVID, rate hikes, the Russian invasion of Ukraine, and other disruptions.

The long-term trend is one of growth but with fluctuations. This can be largely explained by trends in the market.

For example, NAV per share (displayed above as book value per share), declined in 2022, as COVID stimulus halted and as the Federal Reserve began to hike rates. This necessarily reduces the size of a distribution based on a percentage of NAV. The long-term trend has been that NAV per share generally increases over time.

Most entry points in the past decade would enjoy a 10% yield on cost or more if held to the current day, indicating that it's been a fairly effective income vehicle in that regard, even if it entails trimming its capital.

More recently, the S&P 500 has outperformed USA, but there are many bouts in which USA performed better. Thus, it's not only proved to be good for straight income; it's been an effective vehicle for long-term compounding through reinvestment.

Outlook and Valuation

Recently, in addition to the high yield of 11%, USA has been trading at close to a 10% discount to NAV.

The shares often trade near NAV, and discounts like this are not too common. Considering the bearish attitude in markets recently that drove stocks, it's not surprising that USA is lower, particularly if investors expect the stock market to go down more. Treasury Secretary Bessent warned early November that a recession is likely without more rate cuts by the Fed, and many similar sentiments have been felt lately.

While USA doesn't use leverage to enhance its yield, several of their growth stocks (largely those with a tech focus) trade at large multiples, which could easily contract if market sentiment turns more negative. In October, an AI bubble bursting was most-cited risk by fund managers, indicating concerns the ultimate returns of AI capex may not match today's valuations. Equity-focused funds like USA would face a correction if such were to occur.

Take a look at the forward P/Es of their five largest tech holdings.

  • Nvidia (NVDA): 38
  • Microsoft (MSFT): 30
  • Alphabet (GOOG): 30
  • Amazon (AMZN): 33
  • Meta (META): 25

These recently accounted for 17% of the portfolio. All are priced for growth multiples, and those are very sensitive to setbacks, anything that makes the growth less than expected. An AI bubble bursting could do that.

Generally speaking, USA's total return has declined alongside the broader market in these moments.

I can perhaps see a case to reinvest dividends in an existing position at these prices, with the NAV discount especially, but taking an initial position is difficult to support when there's potentially a lot of air to be let out of the tire. I think a stronger NAV discount, as much as 20%, is warranted before an entry seems attractive.

Conclusion

USA is not a bad equity fund, and it functions well. It does not take a great deal of risks, and I think investors have the benefit of reinvesting for total returns like that of the S&P 500 or opting for straight income. I think it's just important to buy well and be mindful that a reasonably managed fund is exposed to market downside risks. For those reasons I give it a Hold rating.

data.title: Charting The Year Ahead: Investment Ideas For 2026
data.description:
Introduction

After a year of broad-based gains, many investors feel both upbeat and uncertain looking toward 2026.

Equities extended their bull run in 2025, but valuations remain historically stretched amid a sharp dispersion of returns across sectors. Cash offered both safety and income for a while, but it no longer seems attractive as the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates. And the U.S. economy appears resilient, yet a K-shaped split shows prosperity for wealthier households diverging from mounting strain for others.

We’ll update our views on the economy and fixed income markets in more detail in our January Cyclical Outlook. For now, we’ll explore select areas of interest across financial markets looking toward 2026 – individual themes that can be brought together in a portfolio – in a way that’s actionable for investors and advisors.

Equities: Expensive on the surface, value beneath

U.S. equities approach 2026 with valuations still near historical highs after a multi-year, technology-driven rally. While AI investment continues to underpin economic growth and market optimism, the concentration of returns in a handful of mega-cap tech stocks raises questions about sustainability.

The tech sector, once celebrated for capital efficiency, has entered a more capital-intensive phase. AI-related spending, previously funded largely by free cash flow, is increasingly fueled by debt issuance. Another notable spending trend: The biggest hyperscalers and chipmakers are funneling billions of their investment dollars into one another through circular deals that amplify sector-specific risks.

Beneath the surface, however, the story is more nuanced. For example, value-oriented stocks remain attractively priced relative to historical averages (see Figure 1), suggesting potential for mean reversion over time.

Figure 1: Value stocks may have more room to run

Macro conditions could provide a tailwind for value in the near term. An outlook for trend-like U.S. economic growth should help broaden earnings growth across sectors in 2026. In our view, the best scenario for value is if the Fed continues cutting rates into reaccelerating and broadening growth.

We also see opportunities to diversify globally. Central banks in emerging markets (EM), having established stronger monetary policy frameworks, now have more flexibility to ease policy and stimulate domestic demand, potentially supporting EM equities. Specifically, we see attractive opportunities in Korea and Taiwan, which offer exposure to the tech sector at cheaper valuations, and China.

Investor takeaway: Focus on value and quality

With high valuations concentrated among a small number of companies, it’s not difficult to find attractively valued stocks with desirable characteristics such as robust balance sheets and healthy growth. Consider tilting toward undervalued sectors rather than chasing the most expensive parts of the market.

Cash is not a strategy: The case for fixed income

In our view, investors who continue holding excess cash today are missing a potential opportunity.

Unusually high returns attracted investors to cash during the post-pandemic period of elevated inflation and Fed interest rate hikes (see Figure 2). But the current Fed rate-cutting phase brings opportunity costs and reinvestment risk, as cash holdings are repeatedly rolled into lower-yielding instruments.

Figure 2: Money market fund assets have kept climbing even as cash rates have declined

As yield curves have steepened, cash yields have declined relative to a variety of bond maturities. Bonds allow investors the potential to lock in income at more favorable levels over longer horizons.

When interest rates fall, cash earns less, but bonds typically gain in value, enhancing total return potential. At today’s yields, high quality bonds look attractive across many possible economic scenarios. With inflation having moved back toward central bank targets, bonds again provide opportunities for diversification through their traditional negative correlation to stocks, helping portfolios weather equity downturns.

Investors can also take advantage of today’s abundance of global fixed income opportunities, with attractive real and nominal yields available in countries across developed and emerging markets, such as the U.K., Australia, Peru, and South Africa (for more, see PIMCO’s October 2025 Cyclical Outlook, “Tariffs, Technology, and Transition”). We believe diversification across regions and currencies is an effective way to harvest differentiated sources of return while fortifying portfolios.

Investor takeaway: Seek to lock in yields with bonds

Consider rotating from cash into high-quality bonds for the potential to lock in yields and position for capital appreciation as interest rates decline. We favor 2- to 5-year bond maturities.

All that glitters: Gold, crypto, and the search for real assets

Gold’s extraordinary rally – recently topping $4,300/oz – has captured widespread attention. Prices have soared to all-time highs even in a generally risk-on market environment. Investor demand for inflation protection, geopolitical hedging, and diversification away from the U.S. dollar has reinforced gold’s role as a strategic asset. Central banks now hold more gold than U.S. Treasuries (see Figure 3), reflecting a shift in reserve management.

Figure 3: Foreign central banks hold more gold than Treasuries

The geopolitical backdrop remains a key driver. The 2022 seizure of Russian reserves helped catalyze gold accumulation as a politically neutral store of value. This trend, coupled with persistent trade frictions and rising sovereign debt, suggests structural support for gold demand. A potential gold price increase of more than 10% over the next year is feasible, in our view.

However, gold’s recent rally has been fueled by momentum and liquidity as much as by fundamentals, and short-term retracements are possible. While falling interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding gold, its valuation appears elevated relative to real yields, warranting careful sizing within portfolios.

Since 2020, commodity indices have delivered returns comparable to global equities but with lower volatility,1 reinforcing their role as diversifiers and inflation hedges. Historical evidence shows that even modest allocations to commodities can improve portfolio efficiency, particularly when inflation runs slightly above central bank targets.

Broad commodities can also provide a potential alternative way to play the AI investment theme, as infrastructure needs drive demand for inputs such as copper, lithium, and energy as well as strategic assets like rare earths.

Crypto assets, led by bitcoin, continue to evolve as digital analogs to gold, appealing to younger investors and those concerned about currency debasement. The recent decline in bitcoin reminds investors that it is a volatile instrument and perhaps not a true store of value. The rise of stablecoins and tokenized assets points to a transformative year ahead for digital finance, though volatility, tax treatment, and regulatory uncertainty remain significant considerations.

Investor takeaway: Enhance diversification and inflation protection

Consider modest, diversified allocations across gold and broad commodities for the potential to enhance portfolio resilience and inflation protection without overconcentrating in any single theme.

Credit markets: Risks and rewards along the credit continuum

Credit spreads remain tight. PIMCO has cautioned throughout 2025 about risks in certain lower-rated credit sectors, particularly within private market areas that have experienced sharp growth. We are now seeing some of those challenges surface, including recent bankruptcies and instances of fraud, which may be symptomatic of broader late-cycle laxity in credit underwriting.

Challenges are showing up in other ways. On average, shares of publicly traded business development companies (BDCs), investment vehicles for corporate direct lending, are trading at roughly a 10% discount to their net asset values. This suggests that the market is cautious about a combination of declining dividends (due to falling short interest rates) and rising credit problems. Indeed, against a strong equity market backdrop, we have also seen share price declines this year for major alternatives firms.

While debt service may become easier with lower interest rates, we have observed more privately financed companies seeking amendments to their loans or paying their debt with additional debt – known as payment-in-kind (PIK) financing – both of which can be signs of debt-servicing challenges (see Figure 4). In fact, using PIK and other data, Lincoln International calculates a “shadow default rate” of 6% as of August 2025, up from 2% in 2021.

Figure 4: Payment-in-kind (PIK) trends can signal debt-servicing challenges

Amid these strains in certain areas, PIMCO sees ongoing opportunities in credit markets for investors who can look beyond whether an investment is public or private (for more, see our latest View From the Investment Committee video, “Starting Valuations Fuel 2025 Bond Performance, 2026 Potential”). In our view, the key is to focus on evaluating liquidity and credit risk across both areas and finding where the potential rewards are greatest.

We are seeing opportunities in certain large-scale financings, where competition is limited; in credit linked to lower-risk consumers; and in select real estate lending. Longer-term trends – such as the buildup of home equity among more affluent borrowers and the expansion of AI and related energy needs – create potential opportunities to provide high quality financing with returns that can rival or exceed those of lower-quality, leveraged corporate credit.

For example, while we have a cautious outlook in general for data centers and AI, many hyperscalers are strong, investment-grade companies that need to invest – and borrow – to build out AI infrastructure. We have seen compelling opportunities in recent months in project finance, or lending secured by data centers that are being built with leases in place to investment-grade tenants. Such financings do not necessarily reflect a sector-level call but rather represent opportunities with high barriers to entry, which can result in attractive valuations, structures, and tenants.

Investor takeaway: Consider active, flexible credit strategies

2026 will likely shed light on whether recent challenges are indicative of broader underwriting problems in lower-rated credit. In the meantime, we would suggest investors think carefully about the positions they hold and whether they are being compensated for their potential credit risk, illiquidity, declining yields, and lack of transparency. The credit continuum offers many opportunities to finely tune exposure to each of these risks, and investors should use the full toolkit. At a time when many direct-lending approaches resemble passive “buy the market” strategies, we believe investors should consider being active in their credit allocations. Consider flexible strategies that are agnostic between public and private in their pursuit of superior risk- and liquidity-adjusted returns.

Conclusion

Across asset classes, a common thread is the need for active decision-making in 2026. Dispersion in equity returns, shifting interest rate dynamics, and the evolving interplay of public and private credit markets underscore the importance of independent investment research and risk management. Rather than chasing crowded trades or relying on static allocations, investors should consider strategies that balance liquidity, return potential, and diversification, while remaining flexible enough to seize new opportunities as they emerge.

Ultimately, 2026 may reward investors who embrace today’s macroeconomic environment: leaning into high quality fixed income as rates decline, selectively adding real assets for resilience amid geopolitical and inflation risks, and identifying undervalued equity sectors amid a concentrated market. In a world where uncertainty persists alongside optimism, thoughtful portfolio construction will be key.

Footnotes

1 Source: Bloomberg and PIMCO data from 31 December 2019 through 30 September 2025. Asset class proxy indices are as follows: commodities – Bloomberg Commodity Index Total Return; gold – Bloomberg Gold Total Return Index; global equities – MSCI World Index – Global Equities.

Disclosures

Past performance is not a guarantee or a reliable indicator of future results.

All investments contain risk and may lose value. Investing in the bond market is subject to risks, including market, interest rate, issuer, credit, inflation risk, and liquidity risk. The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed. Private credit involves an investment in non-publicly traded securities which may be subject to illiquidity risk. Portfolios that invest in private credit may be leveraged and may engage in speculative investment practices that increase the risk of investment loss. Equities may decline in value due to both real and perceived general market, economic and industry conditions. Diversification does not ensure against loss. Commodities contain heightened risk, including market, political, regulatory and natural conditions, and may not be appropriate for all investors. The value of real estate and portfolios that invest in real estate may fluctuate due to: losses from casualty or condemnation, changes in local and general economic conditions, supply and demand, interest rates, property tax rates, regulatory limitations on rents, zoning laws, and operating expenses.

Forecasts, estimates and certain information contained herein are based upon proprietary research and should not be interpreted as investment advice, as an offer or solicitation, nor as the purchase or sale of any financial instrument.
