data.title: Panasonic: Investor Day Disclosures And Housing Business Sale Draw Attention
data.description:
I'm still bullish about Panasonic Holdings (OTCPK:PCRFF) (OTCPK:PCRHY).

I've evaluated its Investor Day disclosures and latest corporate transaction. My take is that PCRFF's outlook is favorable.

The previous December 11, 2024 article discussed my optimistic view of its GenAI- and HVAC-related units.

Investor Event Casts Spotlight On BBU Operations

PCRFF recently hosted an "Investor Relations/IR" Day on Dec 2. The transcript was sourced from S&P Capital IQ.

Management's presentation was focused on its "Battery Backup Unit/BBU" division serving the "Data Center/DC" end-market. This is anticipated to be one of the group's fastest growing and most profitable businesses.

The firm has projected that BBU's turnover will quadruple from slightly below ¥0.2B for FY25 (YE Mar 31, 2025) to more than ¥0.8B in FY29. PCRFF's actual FY2021-2025 revenue CAGR was +6%.

The positive expectations regarding the battery segment are realistic in my opinion.

Around four-fifths of the forecasted BBU revenue for FY2029 is covered by existing "awards." That is defined as "projects agreed upon with customers to advance product development and secure orders for the developed products" by PCRFF.

Also, there is a robust launch pipeline. The company anticipates that 20% of its FY29 BBU-related sales will be derived from "next-generation products" like those used for "power supply racks."

It's important to note that PCRFF is a leader in this huge market. Gartner estimates that the global server industry will expand at an annualized rate of +34% during the 2023-2028 timeframe to $0.22T. The group has a 80% share of DC BBUs according to its own research. These numbers were obtained from its IR Day disclosures.

Separately, PCRFF has described BBU as a "double-digit margin" business at its 2QFY26 analyst briefing on Oct 30 this year. Its group-level EBITDA-to-sales and net margin were 9.4% and 4.0%, respectively on a trailing twelve months' basis.

I think that the battery operations can sustain high profitability.

PCRFF's end-Oct results call commentary emphasized that BBU is "not such an asset-heavy business." This division's capital intensity is at a moderate level, which translates into decent margins.

At the Investor Day, it indicated that the "growth is not just driven by the sales of the (battery) cell as a stand-alone product." Integrated solutions can command a price premium.

PCRFF has also been reconfiguring its existing automotive battery plants to support the increase in DC-focused BBU capacity. That implies a lower amount of capital expenditures is needed to drive future growth.

Housing Subsidiary Divestiture Signals Corporate Restructuring Progress

Last month, PCRFF issued an announcement revealing the move to "transfer 80% of the shares" of "Panasonic Housing Solutions, Co., Ltd. (“PHS”), a wholly-owned" entity to a third party called "YKK Corporation."

I see the above-mentioned as a value-accretive deal.

The divestment was done at an implied EV/EBITDA of 9x, considering PHS's "Enterprise Value/EV" of ¥228B. This is better than PCRFF's forward EV/EBITDA of 6 times (source: S&P Capital IQ).

Its FY2026 operating income is expected to receive a +¥60B boost from the latest transaction. That represented 18% of the consensus's full-year forecast for the company as a whole.

PCRFF's net cash will also rise by ¥0.1T, thanks to PHS's stake reduction.

The Firm's Segments Targeted For Reorganization

As per the chart presented above, "Housing" is among the "low-profit businesses" that it has been planning to deal with.

It's encouraging to learn that PCRFF has taken a big step towards realizing financial improvement by selling the majority of PHS shares.

Earlier, the group has also been successful in turning around some of the other units.

At the 2QFY2026 earnings briefing, PCRFF noted that "we can move the TV business out of the (category of) businesses with issues", and mentioned "the same goes for kitchen appliances."

It has cut the kitchen appliances operations' costs substantially by shifting most of the sourcing and manufacturing to China. Also, the television division benefited from "collaboration with partners" and becoming "as lean as possible" as per the Q2 management call remarks.

Looking ahead, there are opportunities for PCRFF to overhaul the others such as "Industrial Devices", "Electromechanical Control", "Consumer Electronics", and "Air-conditioning."

My prior Dec '24 update touched on how the "HVAC business' future performance" can be enhanced by focusing on "the comparatively higher-margin operations in Europe and Asia (excluding Japan)" and driving down "the proportion of fixed costs."

The sell-side is projecting that PCRFF's EBITDA-to-revenue can go from FY25's 9.8% to 12.6% and 13.5% in FY27 and FY28, respectively as per S&P Capital IQ. This is achievable, taking into account the ongoing business reforms.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

A slower-than-expected pace of DC investments could hurt demand for the company's BBU-related products.

PCRFF's actual margin expansion might disappoint investors. That can happen if it executes poorly on restructuring initiatives.

My Closing Remarks

I've maintained a 'Buy' rating for PCRFF.

I value the stock with the "Gordon Growth Model." My assumptions are a 10% ROE (company's goal), a 9% Cost of Equity (market average), and a +3% Perpetuity Growth Rate (developed economy's expansion rate).

That gives me a target "Price-to-Book" or "P/B" of 1.17 times.

Its current P/B is 0.91x, so the shares can possibly go up by +29% (1.17/0.91).

PCRFF has both growth and profitability levers to justify a re-rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Warning To Young Dividend Investors
data.description:
If you've been a follower of mine, you'll know that I've almost exclusively covered dividend-paying stocks and funds. This includes everything from the traditional blue-chip dividend stocks to the alternative asset classes such as business development companies, REITs, closed-end funds, and now option ETFs. With the growing demand amongst investors for income generation, we've seen a rise in the inflows of capital towards these investment products. While I completely understand the appeal of these funds, I feel an obligation to issue a warning to younger dividend investors that may be making some of the same mistakes that I've lived through.

I was lucky enough to get my first big boy job that had me in a cubicle office at 19 years old. I worked full time and went to school at nights. Since I had an office job a lot earlier than some of my peers, I learned quickly that the corporate world wasn't something that I enjoyed. So I started early with the concept of investing into dividend stocks that can eventually generate enough income to replace a salary. I stayed committed to this process of building a dividend for several years and I eventually worked my way up to a point where my dividend income now pays more than the salary I received at that first job.

The idea was that I wanted to build an income stream that could pay all my expenses, so that I have the opportunity to pursue whatever I wanted. However, the entire way I viewed investing had been wrong. I took a very static approach of focusing on dividend income, despite my life priorities, values, and challenges constantly shifting over the decade. I got older and I eventually figured out what I enjoyed, expenses and life obligations increased, and I no longer had a desire to retire early with dividends.

So as we approach the new year, I have started to reflect back on some of the mistakes I made. I feel that I have an obligation to bring some of these issues to light in an attempt to help some of you younger investors avoid the same mistakes that I made.

Dividends Have Limitations

The beginning premise of building my dividend portfolio was quite simple. I wanted to build a foundation using stocks that I directly consumed every single month. I scrolled social media every single day on my iPhone, so I bought Apple (AAPL). I washed my clothes, brushed my teeth, and cleaned my house using products from Procter & Gamble (PG), so I added that. I'm not a drinker but I loved having the occasional soda, so Coca-Cola (KO) deserved a spot in the portfolio. You get the idea!

Eventually, I accumulated over 25 different positions in dividend companies that consistently produced income for me every month. The only problem was that I was a young guy who wasn't making a lot of money at the time. So my capacity to add capital into these stocks were limited and the process of building this portfolio took a lot longer than I would have liked.

While my dividend portfolio did experience some nice growth over the years, the biggest downfall was that the focus on dividends caused me to under allocate to technology stocks. As you may already know, there are much fewer technology stocks that pay out dividends. As a result, I automatically dismissed many of the companies that aligned with the concept of investing in the things I consume. For instance, here were some of the stocks I dismissed and their respective share prices ten years ago, which didn't pay out dividends a decade ago:

*These numbers are post-splits, going back to 2015 levels

  • Netflix (NFLX): ~$11.89 per share
  • Amazon (AMZN): ~$33.21 per share
  • Alphabet (GOOG): ~$36.94 per share
  • Meta Platforms (META): ~$102.12 per share

The end result is that my portfolio substantially underperformed traditional indexes, such as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100. While I eventually snapped out of it and added some of these companies, I already missed hundreds of percentage points worth of growth. I failed to realize that if I would've simply remained exposed to technology through a straightforward ETF, such as the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), I would've had a lot more money at the end of the road. If I really wanted to still focus on dividends, I could have always sold portions of the gains from these holdings and allocated them to dividend stocks.

While we can always argue that tech's past performance is no indication of the future returns, I think that would be a mistake. Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market is expected to increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. As companies continue to allocate hundreds of billions towards AI infrastructure, it's clear which direction the world is heading. For instance, META confirmed plans to allocate $600B in capex to build out the AI infrastructure through data centers.

Growth Makes Wealth Building Easier

Don't mistake what I am saying! Dividends are still an integral aspect for many investors and I am not oblivious to the use case of them. However, I specifically want younger investors to know that there's a certain tradeoff you are choosing, and perhaps you don't even realize it.

There is a growing popularity in covered call ETFs that still track an underlying index. Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) is one of the most popular examples because you are still getting exposure to constituents of the Nasdaq-100, while also collecting a dividend yield of 9.6%. The issue is that GPIQ's options limit the growth potential of the fund, as I highlight in my recent article. It needs to be understood that a high dividend yield, does not automatically equal a high total return.

Since my portfolio grew at a slower pace than the rest of the market indices, I did a lot of the heavy lifting in order to fuel my growth. I took on new jobs across different industries and put myself through a lot of overtime work in order to make more money. I did this with the sole intention of investing more capital over time.

However, it would've been a lot easier on my body and mind if my portfolio was capturing a greater level of growth, so that I didn't need to do all the heavy lifting on my own. So without even realizing it, I was working myself harder than I probably needed to and I could've eased off the gas a lot sooner if my portfolio was better constructed with growth.

The irony of it all was that I wasn't spending any of the dividends I received anyways. I genuinely took an interest in seeing my wealth grow over time so I always reinvested those dollars. So if I wasn't spending the dividends, we could've made the argument that focusing on growth would have been more beneficial. By focusing on dividends, I was holding a lot of companies that weren't innovating on their products or services, and they were companies that already passed their prime growth spur.

Unexpected Impacts Of Yield Chasing

When I first started including the alternative asset classes, I became obsessed with the high yields that I could receive. REITs like Realty Income (O) offered a starting dividend yield above 5%, while business development companies like Ares Capital (ARCC) offered a dividend yield around 10%. As a result, I had a phase where I over allocated my cash towards these higher yielding positions, which created minimal price growth and the unexpected tax burden.

While I find the concept of these investments really cool, I think the utility is a lot more specific for each investor. If you buy these sort of high yield positions within a regular taxable brokerage account, you will be faced with ordinary dividends, which are taxed at the highest rate for investors. Based on the amount of dividend income you collect from these type of securities, you may be faced with an unexpected tax bill that is much larger than you'd like to pay at the end of the year.

Yield chasing also led me to include assets like MLPs (Master Limited Partnerships), such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). This taught me how much extra work filing my taxes can be, when I need to wait until late March to receive a K-1 form. MLPs can be structured as a pass-through entity, which provides special tax benefits. However, the small income I was receiving from these entities didn't outweigh the burden I faced during tax time.

Takeaway

While I still think dividends are a great aspect to the wealth building experience, I want young investors to avoid a lot of the same mistakes that I made. Sometimes it makes more sense to focus on growth positions that can exponentially increase your wealth faster than you can add new capital over time. By focusing only on dividend-paying companies, you may be aligning your portfolio to underperform traditional indices over time. While the income generated within your portfolio may be giving you a sense of security, it may be coming at the cost of having less money at the end of the road. Yield chasing into higher dividend yield positions can also have some unexpected impacts besides stunted growth, such as a high tax bill and unnecessary paperwork during tax time.

data.title: NextEra: Strong Regulatory Backdrop And Renewables Leadership Support A Higher Target Price
data.description:
After our recent review of the major EU utilities, including Enel and Engie, we are now turning to an update on NextEra (NEE). It has been some time since our last update. Here at the Lab, we were among the few to highlight the link between datacenter expansion and rising power demand (Fig. 2). Yet, the share price and dividends have only delivered a low double-digit return so far (Fig. 1). Given its clear edge in US renewables and its still-compelling valuation, especially when compared with the recent re-rating of EU utilities, we believe the company is well positioned to beat the sector over the coming 12 months. This view is reinforced by solid Q3 results and, more importantly, the new FPL rate agreement.

Fig 1

Source: NextEra Q3 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

We will first touch briefly on the Q3 results, then move on to the latest agreement and our upside case.

The company reported a Q3 2025 adjusted earnings of $1.13 per share compared to a $1.03 result in the prior year quarter (and above the expectation of $1.06) (Fig. 3). Florida Power and Light (FPL) earnings improved to $1.4 billion with an EPS of $0.71. Strong investment activities supported this. On a positive note, NextEra Energy Resources also reported earnings of $1.27 billion and EPS of $0.62. Compared to last quarter, FPL's EPS improved thanks to new investments and higher customer growth (+1.5%), offset by lower usage due to weather and modestly higher usage from a change in retail sales. Adjusted earnings at NextEra Energy Resources were helped by higher customer supply and offset by asset recycling and higher financing costs.

Fig 3

The Florida PSC’s approval of the rate case settlement provides FPL with a very supportive regulatory backdrop, and we see scope for EPS to compound in the high single digits (8%+). Structural demand from data centres should underpin the regulated outlook in Florida and drive further investment in generation and transmission across the US. In our view, a firmer power-price environment helps cushion recontracting risk, while opportunities in NextEra's battery storage, solar, and gas businesses are set to expand.

The commission approved the four-year rate settlement as filed. Key features include:

  • Return framework: an allowed ROE band of 9.95–11.95% with a 59.6% equity ratio.

  • Stabilisation tools: use of around $1.15 billion of deferred tax liabilities and ITCs tied to 522 MW of storage entering service in 2025.

  • Large-load tariff: a dedicated large-customer tariff with a 20-year term and a two-year termination notice.

  • Customer impact: the deal implies roughly a 2% average annual bill increase through 2029, with typical FPL residential bills still about 20% below levels of 20 years ago after inflation adjustment.

  • Rate increases: base-rate hikes of $945 million in 2026 and $705 million in 2027, plus a SOBRA (solar and battery rate adjustment) mechanism for solar (2027–2029) and storage (2028–2029) additions.

Management already previewed these themes at the Edison Electric conference, and we expect the 8 December investor day to elaborate further.

At NextEra, the development pipeline is the largest in the company’s history, with about 30 GW of backlog benefiting from FEOC protection and safe-harbor status, skewed towards solar and storage (Fig. 4). In Q3, the company also added another 3 GW of renewables and storage to this backlog. We think investor expectations for gas generation and infrastructure remain conservative, and NextEra is likely to highlight incremental opportunities in these areas.

Fig 4

The planned restart of the Duane Arnold nuclear plant is expected to add roughly $0.16 EPS on average over the first ten years from 2029 (Fig. 5), and we look for an updated assessment of the upside from expiring PPAs as contracts roll off.

Fig 5

Regarding NextEra Energy Transmission, we expect the company to focus on opportunities in Texas, SPP, and MISO, and there may also be commentary on FERC’s proposed large-load interconnection rule. On funding, as tax credits roll off, the company will need to spell out its capital plan, potentially combining asset recycling, private credit structures, and hybrid/equity-linked instruments.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

The company reaffirmed its EPS guidance for 2025-2026-2027, with yearly midpoints of $3.58, $3.82, and $4.08 (Fig. 6). This implied EPS growth near the high end of the 6-8% range. We see positive developments with Google regarding the restart of the Duane Arnold nuclear facility, and following the Florida PSC’s approval, we believe the company is also de-risked. Finally, we would expect the company to address its backlog of contracts serving technology and data center customers. Continuing to apply a 25x P/E multiple (Fig. 7) to the 2026 EPS of $3.82, we reached a valuation of $95.5 per share (vs our last valuation of $89 per share). Therefore, we continue to buy the company.

Fig 6

Fig 7

Risk

NextEra's downside risks include higher interest rates due to its debt, regulatory changes, and policy risks. The company has a 30 GW backlog, and we recognized the risks of CAPEX inflation, procurement, and poor project execution. There are also risks related to nuclear operations and the gas pipeline. The company is subject to cybersecurity attacks in the transmission grid and extreme weather conditions.

Conclusion

The company is still well placed to capture incremental power demand from AI/data centres, onshoring, and electrification. At the same time, exposure to wind and solar tax credits appears manageable. On the back of the favorable Florida rate decision, we have reiterated our Buy recommendation and lifted our target price.
